schema_note: Published tiers: A_VERIFIED_LOW (ordinal, ranked by $ shortfall vs. 5%-per-year-of-tenure capacity benchmark, uncapped), B_PROBABLY_LOW, C_OPAQUE, ON_TRACK. See docs/methodology.html.
ranking_basis: shortfall_5pct_usd = (net_worth_best × liquidity_pct × 0.05 × years_as_billionaire) − observable_giving_usd. Computed in aggregator, not read from source JSON.
legal_disclaimer: Every figure is an estimate of OBSERVABLE giving from the cited sources. Does not measure total giving. Subjects have been offered a right-of-reply; responses are published on individual profiles. Corrections welcome — see methodology page.

billionaires.wealth_source: Google / Alphabet co-founder (1998); holds ~6% of Alphabet (Class B supervoting + Class C). Also longevity startup investments (Calico funded through Alphabet, not personally philanthropic). Recent AI startup (Dynatomics, reported 2025).
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Page is the canonical Tier A example: 22+ years as billionaire, never signed Giving Pledge, foundation grants are 77-100% DAF-warehoused, direct-to-operating-charity dollars are a small fraction of wealth (<1%), tax-minimization behavior (Fiji, California exit to Miami) not matched by giving velocity. Even accepting foundation grants-out as fully charitable (charitable intent at the foundation level), Page deploys ~0.7% of NW lifetime — an order of magnitude below 10% norm. The DailyMail 0.03% headline is defensible under a strict 'direct to operating charity' numerator; the defensible-generous framing still lands below 1%. Either way: LOW.

foundations_active_names: The Carl Victor Page Memorial Foundation

billionaires.wealth_source: Tesla (CEO, ~13% stake); SpaceX (founder, ~42% stake, private); xAI (founder, ~50% stake); X Corp. (owner); Neuralink; The Boring Company
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Musk is the canonical 'richest person alive who has given least relative to wealth' example. 0.06% lifetime giving rate (Forbes April 2026) is one of the lowest in the Forbes 400. Additional concerns: political giving ($288M+ in 2024) exceeds lifetime philanthropy; Musk Foundation missed IRS 5% minimum three consecutive years; majority of 'giving' goes to Musk-controlled entities (The Foundation school + Fidelity DAF); Giving Pledge signed 2012 remains unfulfilled at 50% target ($405B gap).

billionaires.wealth_source: Founder, chairman, and largest shareholder of Interactive Brokers Group (IBKR); pioneer of electronic securities trading. Concentrated position in IBKR common stock (publicly traded).
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Peterffy is a structural example of the 'private billionaire' archetype: $82.9B fortune, public political engagement ($20M+ GOP), minimal public philanthropy ($49M lifetime observable), no Giving Pledge, no disclosed charitable trust. The Peterffy Foundation is a family vehicle run by his children ($172M assets, $7M/yr out) — impressive in absolute terms, negligible relative to his net worth (0.2% of fortune). Balance for Freedom Inc is effectively dormant. Default prior absent new disclosure: primarily dynastic wealth.

billionaires.wealth_source: News Corp / Fox Corp founder and controlling shareholder via Murdoch Family Trust (Cruden Financial Services, sole trustee). 1999 divorce from Anna Torv converted a portion of holdings into the family trust; 2025 $3.3B settlement restructured beneficial interests.
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see Murdoch as the canonical counterexample to the 'billionaires quietly give a lot' narrative: his personal philanthropy is vanishingly small in both absolute and percentage terms, his foundation is dormant, and he has declined the Giving Pledge. The large News Corp/Fox corporate giving footprint is not his personal wealth. The $3.3B 2025 settlement moved $1.1B into each of three children's hands — family downstream giving (especially Quadrivium) may grow, but that is not Rupert's giving.

billionaires.wealth_source: The Renco Group (private industrial holding company); subsidiaries include AM General (military truckmaker — Humvee), Doe Run Company (lead/zinc), U.S. Magnesium Corp., former WCI Steel. Wealth built via junk-bond financed acquisitions in the 1980s-90s.
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Rennert is a canonical example of 'old private-industrial billionaire, religious-directed giving, no foundation, no pledge, no public philanthropic identity.' This is distinct from dramatic failures-to-give like Ellison — Rennert has quietly given to Jewish causes for decades, probably more than observable, but the scale is an order of magnitude below what a $4B fortune held for 30 years would suggest under any reasonable expected-giving norm.

billionaires.wealth_source: Heir + operator. Chair/CEO/President of Fidelity Investments (FMR LLC); family and affiliates own ~40% of Fidelity. Grandfather Edward C. Johnson II founded Fidelity in 1946. Private company — valuations span widely.
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see Abigail Johnson as the canonical EXAMPLE of why fractional attribution and private-company illiquidity matter. Her family's observable giving ($816M over 6 years via Edward C. Johnson Fund + Fidelity Foundation) is substantial in absolute terms but small relative to $35B net worth. She has NOT signed the Giving Pledge, so there is no public commitment to measure against. Absence of a lever does not mean absence of generosity; it means absence of measurable accountability. Treat as 'quiet — probably mid — unknowable upper bound'.

billionaires.wealth_source: Walmart Inc. — only daughter of founder Sam Walton. Walton family collectively owns ~45% of Walmart through Walton Enterprises LLC and Walton Family Holdings Trust. Wealth held primarily via trust structures rather than direct equity. Also founder/principal of Crystal Bridges Museum of American Art, Art Bridges Foundation, Heartland Whole Health Institute, and Alice L. Walton School of Medicine.
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see Alice Walton as 'the most publicly-visible Walton philanthropist — substantial and recognizable cultural + medical + museum-access giving, with zero officer compensation at her personal vehicles — BUT the foundations are accumulating assets faster than they deploy them (1.1% payout at ALWF 2024 is the signature red flag), and the $134B net worth is compounding faster than the giving. Forbes' own methodology places her in the 1%-4.99% lifetime-giving band. Observable ~$2.2B over 6 years is 0.27%/yr of current NW and 82% of the 10% formal norm. She has NOT signed the Giving Pledge (by family consensus) so cannot be in breach. Comparable reference class: other non-signers with similar wealth — where she looks relatively generous vs. Rob (more observable giving; less political giving) but still well short of the 10% formal norm.

foundations_active_names: Alice L. Walton School of Medicine (AWSOM)
Crystal Bridges Museum of American Art

billionaires.wealth_source: Koch Industries (chairman, co-CEO, majority owner after buying out David Koch estate and other siblings). Privately held — oil refining, chemicals, pipelines, paper, fertilizer, commodity trading. Also significant private-equity and real-estate holdings via Koch Industries.
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see Koch as the canonical political-substitution case. His 501(c)(3) foundation giving is real and tracked. But $5.3B to c4 political advocacy is ~5x all his lifetime charitable giving — a deliberate structural choice. Unlike Ellison (signed Giving Pledge, unfulfilled), Koch never promised and his legacy plan is explicitly c4-centric. Published-list classification MUST distinguish c3 giving from c4 transfers or it will misrepresent his philanthropic profile by 5-10x.

foundations_active_names: Charles J And Elizabeth R Koch Tr Charitable Private Foundation
Stand Together Fellowship (formerly Charles Koch Institute)

billionaires.wealth_source: Continental Resources — founder and executive chairman. 83%+ family stake. Pioneered Bakken shale fracking. Took company private in Nov 2022 via Omega Acquisition Inc. ($4.3B cash to minority shareholders; family pre-owned the rest).
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see Hamm as a case study in 'political-charitable substitution': $4.3M to Trump PACs and $2.2M+ in industry lobbying vehicles (DEPA, Windfall) in parallel with $4.5M HHF distributions in 2024. The 2022 HHF spike ($37.8M) is a one-off around the take-private transaction; it is NOT a sustained giving level. Hamm has signed the Giving Pledge but observable giving is ~0.5% of current net worth.

billionaires.wealth_source: Amazon.com founder (~8.6% stake after post-2019 divorce split with MacKenzie Scott); Blue Origin (100% private); Washington Post (100% private via Nash Holdings)
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Bezos is the canonical example of 'biggest pledge in philanthropic history, behind schedule to deliver.' $2.3B Earth Fund disbursed through 2026 against $10B by 2030 target; $850M Day 1 Fund through 2025 against open-ended $2B; Smithsonian $200M fulfilled. His challenge is not whether he gives — he demonstrably does — but whether the pace matches the pledge and the wealth.

billionaires.wealth_source: Nvidia Corp. co-founder and CEO since 1993; ~3% ownership stake in Nvidia (world's most valuable company as of 2026). Wealth is concentrated in Nvidia stock held in his name, in a family trust, and via the Huang Foundation + GeForce Fund DAF.
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see Huang as a 'recently-minted mega-billionaire whose giving infrastructure has not scaled to match his wealth.' The foundation is actively ramping ($126M in 2024, required ~$369M in 2025). But structural issues remain: 65% of foundation payout goes to his own DAF, not to operating charities; GeForce Fund grantees are not disclosed; LOW_PAYOUT flag is active. Under economic-tenure (3yr) analysis he looks better (ratio 1.4x expected); under formal-tenure analysis he looks worse (0.47x expected). Both views are legitimate. Do NOT classify as 'unfulfilled pledge' — he never signed the Giving Pledge.

foundations_active_names: The Jen-Hsun and Lori Huang Foundation

billionaires.wealth_source: Walmart Inc. — youngest surviving son of founder Sam Walton. Walton family collectively owns ~45% of Walmart. Jim's primary operational vehicle is Arvest Bank (44% personal stake, ~$26B assets, ~$2.3B book value Dec 2025 FDIC filing); served as Arvest CEO until 2024, now Chairman of Arvest Bank Group. Walmart board director 2005-present (joined after brother John's 2005 death). Chairs Community Publishers Inc. (newspaper firm).
billionaires.tier_raw: Probably_Low (Tier B) — trending toward Tier A Verified Low
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see Jim Walton as the canonical example of 'the least visible Walton heir' — a centibillionaire whose primary life's work has been a for-profit regional bank (Arvest), whose personal philanthropic vehicle (Town Branch Foundation, ~$500M endowment, no website) is dwarfed by his brother Rob's RMWF and his sister Alice's Crystal Bridges/AWF. He has not signed the Giving Pledge (by family position) so cannot be 'in breach.' His 2019 $1.2B Walmart-stock gift is the single biggest public philanthropic marker on his record, but the recipient was never disclosed and is most plausibly WFF — making it a transfer-in, not an out-the-door act of generosity to a specific cause. If Jim's goal were to deploy wealth for public good at scale, the mechanisms exist (TBF could grow 10x in a year from a single Walmart-stock contribution); they have not been used.

billionaires.wealth_source: Inherited 42% stake in Koch Industries (second-largest privately held U.S. company, ~$125B annual revenue) from husband David H. Koch upon his death August 23, 2019. Also family-office holdings, NY sports-team stakes (15% BSE Global acquired June 2024 — Brooklyn Nets, NY Liberty; 10% NY Giants acquired October 2025).
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Julia Koch should be presented as the canonical 'estate-transition dropoff' case: when a primary philanthropist (David) dies, the widow-controlled foundation can wind down rather than scale up. Observable giving post-2019 is a small fraction of David's pre-2019 ~$30-60M/yr rate, despite Julia's net worth being multiples higher than David's was. Also: she uses (c)(4) vehicles rather than (c)(3) foundations, which creates opacity and is structurally identical to the pattern flagged in Charles Koch's profile.

billionaires.wealth_source: Founder and CEO of Citadel LLC (hedge fund, ~$69B AUM) and Founder/Non-Executive Chairman of Citadel Securities (market maker). Both privately held.
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Griffin is a useful contrast to Ellison: HIGH observable dollars, but HIGH political dollars and explicit refusal to sign a percentage pledge. On the Scrooge List frame he is NOT a canonical 'Scrooge' — he gives real money — but his score is pulled up by political substitution and the opacity of his DAF-channel giving.

foundations_active_names: Citadel Group Foundation (a.k.a. The Citadel Foundation)

billionaires.wealth_source: Oracle Corp. co-founder; 41% ownership stake; also significant Tesla holdings and Lawrence Investments family office
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see Ellison as the canonical example of 'signed the Giving Pledge, hasn't followed through yet.' Observable giving ~$467M is 0.2% of current net worth and 8% of the 10% formal norm. The trust could still make good; as of 2026-04, it has not.

billionaires.wealth_source: Broadcast.com co-founder (sold to Yahoo! 1999 for $5.7B in stock); Dallas Mavericks owner 2000-2023 (sold 73% majority stake to Adelson/Dumont families Dec 2023 for ~$3.5B); Shark Tank investor; Mark Cuban Cost Plus Drug Company (PBC, 2022-).
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Cuban is the canonical 'public-facing generous persona, light documented giving' case. Readers who follow him on Shark Tank / X would guess he gives ~$100M+/year; the 990-PF record shows MCF averaged ~$4M/year 2019-2024. The hidden upper bound plausibly reaches ~$200M cumulative — respectable but still below 1% of current net worth after 26 years as a billionaire. Rhetoric-action gap is real; the scale is smaller than Ellison's but in the same direction.

foundations_active_names: Mark Cuban Heroes Recreational Centers Foundation

billionaires.wealth_source: Las Vegas Sands Corp. — majority stake inherited from husband Sheldon G. Adelson (died Jan 2021). Also: co-owner Dallas Mavericks (Dec 2023, ~69% via family), publisher Israel Hayom, co-owner Las Vegas Review-Journal.
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Miriam Adelson presents the canonical POLITICAL_SUBSTITUTION case. Observable charitable giving is roughly at or slightly above the strict 10% norm, BUT she simultaneously deploys equal or greater dollars on partisan political influence which under NBER W26616 displaces ~33 cents of charitable per dollar political. For readers: her ~$840M observable charitable would likely be ~$890M+ absent the ~$156M+ recent political giving. The Giving Pledge absence despite decades as top-50-billionaire household is the stronger critique than the observable-ratio itself.

foundations_active_names: Dr Miriam and Sheldon G Adelson Medical Research Foundation

billionaires.wealth_source: Oil & gas (Anschutz Ranch field, sold interest to Mobil 1982 for $500M); Union Pacific/Southern Pacific railroad stakes; The Anschutz Corporation (private holdco) — AEG (Anschutz Entertainment Group, owns Coachella/LA Kings/LA Galaxy/O2 Arena), Clarity Media (Washington Examiner), Regal Entertainment, Xanterra Parks & Resorts, Walden Media
billionaires.tier_raw: Probably_Low_To_Baseline (Tier B, leaning C on full data pull)
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Anschutz is the opposite of the 'signed the pledge, hasn't followed through' case (Ellison). He did NOT sign the Giving Pledge and therefore made no public commitment, but he has consistently deployed $50-82M/yr through his foundation for 40+ years, plus $300M+ to a named medical campus. The legitimate Scrooge concerns here are: (1) ideology of recipients (anti-LGBTQ-adjacent Christian causes), (2) low payout rate several recent years (3.9-4.7%), (3) politics-over-charity displacement signaled by $1.9M 2024 political giving. None of these make him a quantitative Scrooge — they make him an ideologically contentious philanthropist.

billionaires.wealth_source: Walmart Inc. — eldest son of founder Sam Walton; Walmart chairman 1992-2015, chairman emeritus thereafter. Walton family collectively owns ~45% of Walmart. Also principal owner of Denver Broncos (2022 purchase for $4.65B), 25.5% stake in Arvest Bank (~$2.3B book).
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see Rob Walton as the canonical example of 'old-money dynasty — substantial joint-family foundation giving, no Giving Pledge signature, surging political giving, and individual wealth compounding faster than individual generosity.' Observable giving ~$1.5B over 6 years is 0.18%/yr of current NW and 53% of the 10% formal norm. He has NOT signed the Giving Pledge (by choice) so cannot be 'in breach' of it — the comparable reference class is other non-signers with similar wealth and similar observable giving rates.

billionaires.wealth_source: Alphabet / Google co-founder; large Class B and Class C Alphabet stake; Bayshore Global Management (family office) manages non-Alphabet holdings including Tesla stock
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Brin is the canonical case of 'real giving but still small relative to the pile.' Observable ~$2.4B is ~1% of current net worth and ~22% of the 10% formal norm. His Parkinson's commitment is genuine and historically significant — he is, factually, the largest individual donor to Parkinson's research ever. But that commitment now represents a tiny fraction of his wealth. Trajectory matters: if 2025's $2B in stock transfers disburses on 2024's $722M/yr cadence, his ratio will improve meaningfully in coming years.

billionaires.wealth_source: Related Companies (real estate development — Hudson Yards, Deutsche Bank Center/Time Warner Center); Miami Dolphins NFL team (~94% stake); Equinox Holdings (SoulCycle, Equinox Fitness); Related Ross (West Palm Beach); Formula 1 Miami Grand Prix stake
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Ross is not a classic Scrooge case. He has given $478M lifetime to University of Michigan alone (largest donor in UM history), $100M cumulative to WRI, $30M to RISE, $100M to DCI. The Scrooge lens flags him because: (1) signed Giving Pledge but hasn't approached the 'substantial majority' threshold given his $17B fortune, (2) heavy political-fundraiser role for Trump, (3) wealth is locked in illiquid sports/real estate assets, (4) 2024 foundation payout anomaly.

billionaires.wealth_source: Blackstone Inc. co-founder, chairman & CEO; owns ~19% of publicly-traded Blackstone; also large annual compensation/dividend income ($1.2B in 2025)
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Schwarzman is the canonical 'named gift' philanthropist. Observable $1.05B is ~2% of current net worth and 55% of the 10% formal norm. He has given substantially more than Ellison or Zuckerberg observably but is pursuing the classical posthumous-foundation strategy Buffett critiqued. Political giving ($78M+ since 2020) approaches philanthropic giving on an annual basis.

billionaires.wealth_source: Microsoft — joined 1980 as employee #30, granted 8% equity stake by Bill Gates; CEO 2000-2014; retains ~4% ownership (~333M shares). Also LA Clippers (purchased 2014 for $2B; Sportico valuation ~$6.72B Oct 2025) and The Forum.
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Ballmer is the canonical 'probably giving a lot, but we can't independently verify' case. On self-reported + Forbes-reconciled figures, he is giving at 70-80% of the 10% formal norm — among the more generous top-15 billionaires. But the LLC structure means that if self-reporting stopped tomorrow, we would have very little ability to reconstruct the giving from public records. Readers should see Ballmer as 'transparency mode choice' — a billionaire who gives meaningfully but has chosen a vehicle class that lets him stop disclosing whenever he wants.

billionaires.wealth_source: Hedge fund founder — SAC Capital Advisors (1992-2016); Point72 Asset Management (2014-present, ~$45.7B AUM). Owner of NY Mets (acquired controlling stake Sept 2020 for $2.4B). Major art collector.
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Cohen is the canonical example of 'built genuine infrastructure — CVN mental-health clinics, LaGuardia workforce center, MoMA capital — without Giving Pledge signaling.' Observable giving ratio 3.5% of current net worth is an order of magnitude higher than Ellison's 0.2%. Readers should not confuse the absence of a pledge with absence of giving. Flag the SAC history and political-donation defensiveness for context, not as giving-denominator adjustments.

billionaires.wealth_source: Media/internet mogul. Chairman & Senior Executive of IAC/InterActiveCorp and Expedia Group. Prior career: Paramount Pictures Chairman/CEO 1974-1984, Fox Broadcasting Company co-founder 1984-1992, QVC/USA Broadcasting. Spouse: fashion designer Diane von Furstenberg (m. 2001); owns ~1/3 of DVF's eponymous fashion company.
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Diller is the counter-example to the pledge-unfulfilled archetype: modest-by-billionaire-standards net worth, roughly on pace with his Giving Pledge commitment, concrete large-dollar disbursements. The critique is qualitative (cause selection) not quantitative. Readers should understand 'Tier C / Probably Adequate' as 'not a Scrooge' not as 'exemplary.'

billionaires.wealth_source: Saban Entertainment (Power Rangers); $1.6B+ personal profit from 2001 Disney acquisition of Fox Family Worldwide; lead investor in Univision Communications buyout consortium (2007); Saban Capital Group family office with media and financial services holdings
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Saban is an interesting control case: proves the Scrooge List isn't just 'didn't sign the Giving Pledge = Tier A.' Saban didn't sign and still gives above the 10% norm. Flag him as 'Moderate Giver, politically-heavy, policy-influence-heavy' — not a Scrooge target. Core narrative: the Giving Pledge is one signal but not the only one.

billionaires.wealth_source: Cable TV (TCI, sold to AT&T 1999); Liberty Media / Liberty Global / Warner Bros. Discovery / QVC Group tracking stocks; also ranching and real estate (Silver Spur Ranches, Bar Lazy Seven, etc.) — largest private landowner in US from 2011-2021 (~2.2M acres), since surpassed to #3
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Malone is the archetype of the 'low-key private giver with big stated intentions.' Unlike Ellison (signed pledge, massive wealth, clearly not tracking), Malone never signed a pledge, his wealth has been stable ($8-12B) for 25+ years, and he has given ~$290M observably plus invested $23M+ in Greenland and stated intent to conserve 2.2M acres. The key uncertainties: (a) will the land-trust intent be executed, and (b) at what appraised value. Without those, he's closer to Tier B 'Probably Mid' than A or D.

billionaires.wealth_source: Inherited from Steve Jobs (d. Oct 2011) via Steven P. Jobs Trust — principally Apple shares and 7.3% Disney stake from Pixar sale. Holdings diversified post-2011 (half Disney stake sold 2017 for ~$7B pre-tax; annual ~10% Apple sell-down through 2021). Operates Emerson Collective LLC as unified investment + philanthropy + media vehicle.
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see LPJ as the canonical 'opaque LLC' case alongside CZI. Observable Waverley 990-PF giving ($1.22B since 2019) meets the formal 10%-of-liquid expectation. Her public statements and the $3.5B Waverley climate pledge indicate intent to give substantially more. But Emerson Collective LLC's grant pipeline is structurally invisible, and her $28B-AUM LLC could be deploying $0 or $10B+ in true charitable flow with no way to distinguish from outside. This is a METHODOLOGY problem, not a moral judgment: the U.S. tax code permits this opacity.

foundations_active_names: Waverley Street Foundation (formerly Emerson Collective Foundation)

billionaires.wealth_source: Access Industries (founded 1986). Fortune built through post-Soviet aluminum (Sual/Rusal) and oil (Tyumen Oil / TNK-BP joint venture with Viktor Vekselberg and Mikhail Fridman's Alfa Group; sold 50% stake to Rosneft in 2013 for ~$28B gross, ~$7B personal). Now chairman of Warner Music Group (acquired 2011), ~21% stake in LyondellBasell, owner of DAZN, significant real estate and venture portfolio.
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see Blavatnik as the canonical example of 'significant named giving to elite institutions (Oxford, Harvard, Yale, Tate, TAU) concurrent with wealth sourced from post-Soviet aluminum/oil privatizations and a $7B TNK-BP sale to Rosneft.' The scale of giving ($800M+ observable) is real; the reputation-laundering allegation is documented in credible press and cited here without Scrooge adjudicating it. Not a Scrooge, not a saint — a legitimate data point readers can interpret.

foundations_active_names: Warner Music Group Blavatnik Family Foundation Social Justice Fund

billionaires.wealth_source: Meta Platforms co-founder & CEO; ~13% ownership stake (as of Nov 2025 Bloomberg filing); also Zuckerberg private investments / family office
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Zuckerberg is the canonical LLC_OPACITY case. His 990-PF grants alone (~$1.87B 2017-2024) would place him mid-tier. The 99% Meta shares pledge, if fully disbursed to 501(c)(3)s over his lifetime, would make him one of the largest philanthropists in history. As of 2026-04, only ~2.3% of that notional has reached the 501(c)(3) arm of CZI. The rest sits in a for-profit LLC whose grantees we cannot independently verify. Readers should understand this as: 'Signed the Giving Pledge, structured primary giving vehicle as a for-profit LLC, self-reports large aggregate giving — external verification confirms less than 30% of the self-report.'

billionaires.wealth_source: Entertainment industry founder — Asylum Records, Geffen Records (sold to MCA 1990 for ~$550M in shares, ~$670M from Matsushita's 1991 MCA acquisition), DGC Records, Geffen Pictures, co-founder DreamWorks SKG (1994) with Spielberg and Katzenberg. Major art collection (reported $2.3B). Substantial real estate and Warner Bros. Discovery stake.
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Geffen is on track on dollars-out — observable giving exceeds the 10% norm. Readers should understand the giving is heavily directed at prestige naming opportunities at wealthy institutions (UCLA, Yale, MoMA, LACMA, Lincoln Center, MSK). Impact-per-dollar is a separate question from quantity-given. The Scrooge List measures the latter; the PATTERN flags surface the former for reader judgment.

billionaires.wealth_source: Carlyle Group co-founder (1987); private equity carried interest; Declaration Partners family office (~$2B AUM, 2017-); principal owner Baltimore Orioles (2024-)
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Rubenstein presents a methodology edge case: by raw dollars-given / wealth ratio he is Tier C ('On Track'), but the POLITICAL_PATRIOTIC_PR_PATTERN red flag raises a legitimate question about NET civic contribution when carried-interest lobbying is netted against monument giving. The Scrooge List convention (per CLAUDE.md) is to use raw observable ratios for tier assignment and surface the behavioral pattern via red_flags rather than re-tiering. Readers should see both signals: (a) Rubenstein gives at a higher % of wealth than ~95% of US billionaires, AND (b) his specific pattern of small-high-visibility gifts is the textbook case study for the Patriotic Millionaires critique of elite philanthropy.

foundations_active_names: Rubenstein Foundation (David B Rubenstein Et Al Ttee)

billionaires.wealth_source: Facebook (Meta) co-founder; Asana co-founder and former CEO; residual Meta holdings plus Asana equity drive net worth
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see Moskovitz/Tuna as the canonical example of 'signed the Giving Pledge young and actually delivered.' Cumulative $5B in observable grants + $7.94B committed inside a transparent foundation + explicit spend-down intent + ~$10B already moved out of personal balance sheet into the philanthropic orbit. At $10.2B current personal net worth, they have given away or committed roughly double their Forbes personal net worth to charity already.

billionaires.wealth_source: Google/Alphabet — CEO 2001-2011, Executive Chairman 2011-2017; ~1% economic interest in Alphabet plus extensive private venture portfolio (Hillspire family office, Innovation Endeavors VC). Current CEO of Relativity Space (2025-).
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Schmidt is the canonical example of 'wealthy technologist giving at a pace consistent with the 10% norm, through a mix of 501(c)(3) foundations AND an LLC.' Two caveats worth flagging in the published profile: (1) The LLC portion (Schmidt Futures via Futures Action Network) has opaque grantee lists — we cannot verify what share of LLC spend hits verifiable charities vs. for-profit impact investment vs. policy influence. (2) Significant portion of his giving flows to science policy infrastructure (SCSP, NSCAI work) where 'philanthropy' and 'policy influence' overlap — generosity tier is defensible, but the 'to whom' question deserves transparency.

foundations_active_names: The Eric and Wendy Schmidt Fund for Strategic Innovation

billionaires.wealth_source: BOK Financial Corp. (61.5% owner; $46B-asset bank); Kaiser-Francis Oil Company (private E&P, 23rd largest non-public U.S. energy firm); Excelerate Energy (82M shares, LNG); Oklahoma City Thunder minority stake (2014)
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Kaiser is the 'model pledge signer that delivers' case in this cohort — alongside the Arnolds, Moskovitz/Tuna, Laurene Powell Jobs. Caveats worth surfacing in profile: (a) supporting-organization structure is a real structural critique that the Senate Finance Committee flagged in 2005 and that has not gone away; (b) Solyndra is the dominant ethics-adjacent story; (c) Tulsa geographic concentration limits cause-optimization by GiveWell-style frameworks but is Kaiser's explicit intent.

billionaires.wealth_source: KKR & Co. co-founder (1976) and co-executive chairman; pioneer of leveraged buyouts. Wealth is concentrated in KKR stock plus decades of fund carry distributions.
billionaires.tier_reasoning: The headline reading: Kravis gives more observably than most mega-rich peers, through a family foundation that is visible on 990-PF filings. Limits of this reading: (a) foundation is a pass-through with low endowment — if Kravis stops contributing, grants stop; (b) ~$9B of his KKR stake is held via opaque trust/LLC structures whose charitable disposition is unknown; (c) he did NOT sign the Giving Pledge and has made no public multi-decade total-fortune commitment; (d) political giving and fossil-fuel investment context make him a polarizing figure despite strong observable philanthropy.

foundations_active_names: The Marie-Josée and Henry R. Kravis Foundation

billionaires.wealth_source: eBay founding president and first full-time employee (1996-2001); cashed out ~$2B of eBay stock post-1998 IPO. Current holdings via Capricorn Investment Group (family office, ~$5B AUM, ESG/impact-focused). Wealth has DECLINED from a peak as a result of philanthropic disbursements and Participant Media losses.
billionaires.tier_raw: On_Track (Tier A equivalent — model pledge signer)
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Skoll should be published as a positive example: declining net worth, no red flags, 26.7% of current wealth already given, pledge on credible trajectory. Contrast directly with Ellison in profile copy. Only soft concern is HIGH_OFFICER_COMP at Skoll Foundation ($853K CEO) — worth noting but not disqualifying.

billionaires.wealth_source: Omega Advisors hedge fund founder (1991-2018, converted to family office); prior 25-year career at Goldman Sachs, rose to chairman/CEO of Goldman Sachs Asset Management
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Cooperman is the canonical example of 'Giving Pledge signer who is actually giving.' Net worth is small ($3.3B vs. Ellison's $227B), which makes the pledge target reachable within an ordinary human lifetime. Public persona as philanthropy-defender-in-chief aligns with observable disbursements. The structural risk is dynasty-foundation compounding rather than lifetime deployment — watch LTCFF payout rate over the next decade.

foundations_active_names: Leon and Toby Cooperman Family Foundation

billionaires.wealth_source: The Estée Lauder Companies — eldest son of Estée and Joseph H. Lauder; president 1972-1995, CEO 1982-1999, Chairman Emeritus thereafter. Primary asset: Estée Lauder Cos. (EL) stock via family holdings; Lauder family controls multi-class voting shares.
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Leonard Lauder is the canonical example of 'art-heavy lifetime giving that appears ON-TRACK even though the headline number depends on a single appraisal-based non-cash gift.' Readers should see his $1.1B Met Cubist collection as an IMPORTANT but CONTINGENT piece of the observable total — a stricter valuation approach would materially shift the ratio.

billionaires.wealth_source: Walmart Inc. — third-generation heir; grandson of founder Sam Walton; only child of John T. Walton (died 2005 plane crash) and Christy Walton. Inherited the bulk of his father's estate (approximately one-third per some sources; 'biggest beneficiary' per Bloomberg court-document analysis). Holds ~3.7% Walmart stake. Also founded S2G Ventures (2014, food/ag impact VC; spun out as RIA May 2024) and serves as Executive Chairman/Founder of Builders Vision, his family office/impact platform managing ~$15B across venture capital, philanthropy (Builders Initiative), and sustainable asset management (Builders Asset Management).
billionaires.tier_raw: On_Track (Tier C) or borderline Probably_Low (Tier B)
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see Lukas Walton as 'the third-generation Walton who broke the mold — built a purpose-driven $1.65B impact platform, kept political giving modest and Democratic-leaning, focused on climate/oceans/food, but whose observable 990-PF grants ($340M over 7 years) still lag his wealth ($40B) by a factor of 100 over that period.' The HEADLINE figures ($3B deployed, $2B committed, $15B platform) conflate grants with for-profit impact investment — use the foundation's 990-PF grants_out line for strict comparison. He is NOT a Giving Pledge signer (contra the research brief) — the family's collective declination applies to him per the known record. He chairs WFF's Environment Program Committee and has personally contributed $149M to WFF (transfer_in). On the 10% formal norm he's tracking below capacity but improving faster than his peers. Best-case Tier C, most-likely borderline Tier B.

billionaires.name_legal: MacKenzie Scott (formerly MacKenzie Bezos; formerly MacKenzie Tuttle)
billionaires.wealth_source: Amazon divorce settlement 2019 — received ~25% of the couple's Amazon shares (~4% of Amazon outstanding, ~19.7M shares at settlement), tax-free under IRC §1041. No operating-company role. Author. Post-divorce wealth managed through family office Lost Horse LLC (Seattle, est. late 2019).
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Scott is the canonical methodological edge case for the Scrooge List: she is simultaneously one of the MOST observed (detailed grant-recipient database) and one of the MOST opaque (no foundation, undated DAF inflows, family-office LLC) billionaires in the US cohort. Her exclusion from Chronicle of Philanthropy's Philanthropy 50 (2024, 2025, 2026) despite giving more than the list's #1 ranked donor in 2025 ($7.1B vs. Bloomberg's $4.3B) is, by itself, evidence that standard philanthropy-tracking infrastructure is broken. The scrooge-list methodology choice to credit Scott's self-published grants-to-501(c)(3) totals is a deliberate departure from Chronicle methodology and should be flagged in methodology prose.

billionaires.wealth_source: Divorce settlement from Bill Gates (Cascade Investment transferred ~$2.4B in securities in May 2021 — AutoNation, Canadian National Railway, Coca-Cola FEMSA, Grupo Televisa); plus $12.5B received from Gates Foundation in 2024 upon resignation as co-chair, earmarked by her for women-and-families philanthropy. No operating-company stake of her own — wealth is entirely a derived/transferred position from her ex-spouse's Microsoft fortune.
billionaires.tier_raw: ON_TRACK (Tier C — publicly committed and actively deploying)
billionaires.tier_reasoning: MFG is a counterexample to the pledge-unfulfilled pattern: she co-founded the Giving Pledge, publicly criticizes laggards (Dec 2025 Fortune), and has deployed $487M in 2024 alone. Main observability concern is the Pivotal Ventures LLC opacity — some unknown portion of her giving is invisible to 990 reporting. On balance: ON_TRACK, NOT a Scrooge candidate.

billionaires.wealth_source: Bloomberg L.P. (financial data/media terminal, founded 1981) — sole owner of ~88% of the company; also former 3-term mayor of New York City
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Bloomberg is the reference point for 'what a centibillionaire actively fulfilling the pledge looks like' — high base rate of disclosed giving (~$3.7B/yr, $21.1B cumulative), structural Bloomberg L.P. succession commitment, clean foundation governance (12%+ payout, nominal comp), no red flags. His observable/pledge-target ratio (~19%) is ~95× Ellison's (~0.2%). Use Bloomberg to calibrate the upper boundary of the Scrooge tier system. Any scoring formula that classifies Bloomberg as 'Scrooge-like' is prima facie miscalibrated.

foundations_active_names: Bloomberg Family Foundation Inc. (dba Bloomberg Philanthropies)

billionaires.wealth_source: Dell Technologies founder/CEO (~45-50% stake); also Broadcom stake (from 2023 VMware sale to Broadcom for $68B); DFO Management / MSD Capital family office; Secureworks stake
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see Dell as the anti-Ellison: did NOT sign Giving Pledge but HAS built a serious working foundation over 25 years, zero-comp principals, broad operations. Key tensions: (1) $5B endowment injection accumulating faster than deploying (low payout ratio watch); (2) $210M DAF transfer opacity; (3) $6.25B Trump Accounts pledge is NOT conventional charity. Dell is notable for doing real philanthropy without the public commitment apparatus (Pledge, LLC, splashy vehicles).

billionaires.wealth_source: Nike, Inc. co-founder (1964, originally Blue Ribbon Sports); largest shareholder and chairman emeritus. Also holds Laika (stop-motion animation studio, owned via son Travis Knight) and other private investments.
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see Knight as the counterexample to Ellison: no Giving Pledge signature, but substantially MORE observable giving delivered ($3.28B vs $0.47B) at a much smaller net worth ($35B vs $227B). Knight demonstrates that pledge-signing is neither necessary (he hasn't signed) nor sufficient (Ellison has signed, minimally delivered). On-track classification should be qualified by: (1) foundation payout sitting at the 5% legal minimum, and (2) the $2B OHSU pledge is a multi-year commitment at age 88.

foundations_active_names: Knight Foundation (Phil & Penny Knight private foundation, Beaverton OR)

billionaires.wealth_source: eBay founder (1995); remaining ~4% eBay + ~5% PayPal (post-2015 spinoff) stakes; also Omidyar Network hybrid philanthropy/investment platform
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see Omidyar as: (1) a transparent-LLC example showing the structure itself isn't the problem — CZI/Ballmer could disclose like this and choose not to; (2) a Giving Pledge signer whose net worth has actually DECLINED from peak, one of very few for whom this is true; (3) 'on track' not 'fulfilled' — giving is ~17% of fortune per IPS 2025, pledge target is 50%+, still meaningful gap remaining.

foundations_active_names: First Look Institute Inc (formerly First Look Media Works)

billionaires.wealth_source: Bridgewater Associates — founder 1975, world's largest hedge fund at peak (~$124B AUM). Sold his last shares of Bridgewater in July 2025 for ~$6.8B cash. Remaining wealth is diversified across cash, family office holdings, and real estate.
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see Dalio as a signed Giving Pledge member who is materially delivering on the commitment. Key nuances: (1) the $7B+ 'Dalio Philanthropies' lifetime figure includes transfers INTO the foundations and family fund, not just grants OUT — actual charitable deployment is lower. (2) LOW_PAYOUT flag on Dalio Foundation Inc 2021-2024 is real — the foundation has ballooned from $740M to $1.67B in assets while grant cadence slowed. (3) Connecticut partnership governance collapse in 2020 is a genuine governance flag. (4) Observable giving is understated in this file because Dalio Family Fund Inc 990s are not yet retrieved.

billionaires.wealth_source: Netflix Inc. co-founder (1997); served as CEO 1999-2023; Executive Chairman 2023-2026; announced exit from Netflix board April 2026. Wealth now concentrated in remaining Netflix stock (<1% of outstanding after 2024 gifts + sales) plus diversified liquid assets. Former Pure Software founder (sold 1997).
billionaires.tier_raw: Opaque (Tier C) OR On Track (Tier B) — methodology-dependent
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Hastings is the canonical 'DAF-heavy Giving Pledger' — publicly committed, structurally liquid, and has moved large dollar amounts out of his personal balance sheet into a charitable vehicle. Readers should know that $1.7B has been committed but only ~$200M has been verifiably delivered to end recipients as of 2026-04. The DAF may be disbursing at a healthy rate; we simply can't see it. This is a transparency concern, not (necessarily) a generosity concern.

billionaires.wealth_source: Co-founder and Executive Chairman of Kinder Morgan Inc. (KMI) — North America's largest energy infrastructure / pipeline operator. Majority of fortune from ~12% stake in KMI. Co-founded with William Morgan in 1997 after leaving Enron as president/COO.
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see Kinder as the canonical example of 'signed the Giving Pledge, actually doing it.' Observable giving exceeds the 10% formal norm. The remaining 93% of wealth is committed via estate bequest to an existing operating foundation — this is higher-credibility than opaque trust structures. Main uncertainty: whether lifetime vs. estate split is suboptimal (estate-only giving delays impact by decades). Counterfactual: if Kinder stopped giving today, Houston loses its largest private parks/arts/education funder.

billionaires.wealth_source: Duquesne Capital (founded 1981, closed 2010 with $12B AUM); former lead portfolio manager at Soros Fund Management (1988-2000, including the 1992 Black Wednesday sterling trade); now runs Duquesne Family Office (~$10.8B AUM per Bloomberg Dec 2024)
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Druckenmiller is the structural counter-example to the Giving Pledge signers clustering in Tier B/C. He declined to sign the Pledge, yet has exceeded what the 10% norm expects of him. Observable giving is 6.6% of current net worth AND represents a still-growing foundation endowment — the $1.77B foundation assets will disburse regardless of his personal spending decisions.

billionaires.wealth_source: Berkshire Hathaway — chairman, CEO (stepping down end of 2025) and largest individual shareholder; essentially 100% Berkshire common stock at time of 2006 pledge (98%+ of net worth)
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Use Buffett as the anchor/ceiling in lists and methodology pages: 'Buffett's $60B+ lifetime giving, with 99.5% of remainder committed at death, is the reference standard by which we judge other pledge signers.' Ellison gap to Buffett-equivalent = 99.8% short. A billionaire's position on the Scrooge list is effectively measured by distance from Buffett.
